lessonsLearned.lesson: Every piece of context must have exactly one owner. When ownership is ambiguous, context drifts silently.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Apply when creating or reviewing any context data point

lessonsLearned.lesson: Context rot is the natural state. Without active maintenance, all business context degrades over time.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Use this as motivation for regular maintenance cycles

lessonsLearned.lesson: The fastest way to detect context rot is to find the same information in two places and check if they still agree.
Start with 3-5 data points. Add more only after the first set is actively maintained and useful.
Infrastructure must be wired into workflow steps to be useful. A maintenance checklist that nobody follows is worse than no checklist.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When designing or reviewing maintenance processes

lessonsLearned.lesson: When the same concept has different names in different data points, comparison and analysis become unreliable.
lessonsLearned.applicability: During context reviews and cross-data-point audits

lessonsLearned.lesson: Consolidate AFTER pattern is proven stable. Premature abstraction creates complexity without value.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When deciding whether to merge or restructure data points

lessonsLearned.lesson: Reference the source, don't copy it. When you need information from another data point, link to it instead of duplicating the content.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When writing data point content that touches another data point's domain

lessonsLearned.lesson: Discovery scripts are the source of truth for what exists. State files record what discovery found, not the definition itself.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When checking ecosystem inventory or resolving discrepancies

lessonsLearned.lesson: A single translation map (technical-id -> human-label) lets the entire dashboard switch audience tier in one commit. Collectors keep emitting raw IDs; renderers prefer the display_* twin and fall back to raw. The biggest UX wins came from this one file (labels.py), not from rewriting individual panels.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When a UI surfaces internal identifiers (job IDs, enums, codes) to a non-developer audience, route every label through a single SoT module before per-component polish

lessonsLearned.lesson: A clear-planner manifest (plan-manifest.json + implementation-plan.md with explicit task IDs P1_001, P2_007, ...) makes a 38-task UX rebuild fully resumable in a fresh session with zero conversation context. The single resumption prompt 'Read plan-manifest at <path>, start at P1_001' was sufficient to ship Steps 4-7 across multiple sessions.
lessonsLearned.applicability: For any multi-step refactor expected to span sessions or get interrupted, write the plan as a manifest with task-IDs and a self-contained resumption guide

lessonsLearned.lesson: When one panel composes others (e.g. cockpit composes jobs_panel + actions_inbox), every mutation handler must invalidate BOTH the directly-touched panel AND every panel that composes it. Otherwise the periodic /api/data refresh re-renders the composing panel from a stale cache and overwrites optimistic UI changes.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Whenever a dashboard collector reads from another collector's cached output. Audit POST handlers for every panel ID in the read chain, not just the one whose data the user mutated.

lessonsLearned.lesson: A state file that exists to mirror disk should be regenerated from disk on a regular cadence, not authored by hand or merged additively. When treated as authored truth, it drifts the moment someone adds a directory and forgets to update the JSON — and the gap compounds invisibly until an audit catches it weeks later. The fix is to make the file a derived artifact: refreshed at install (update.py), daily (index-health), and as a whitelisted auto-fix (architecture-review). This concretizes lesson L-INIT-20260404-009 ('Discovery scripts are the source of truth') into ecosystem state.json specifically. Counter-example: lessons.json IS authored truth, so it stays excluded from auto-refresh.
lessonsLearned.applicability: When designing any registry, cache, or inventory file that mirrors a queryable source (filesystem, database, API). Default to regenerate-from-source unless the file is genuinely authored. If derived, ship a deterministic regenerator script and wire it into the install path AND a periodic refresh.

lessonsLearned.lesson: For multi-phase capability work, make the plan manifest itself part of the regression suite. A test that expects completed task IDs to be marked completed catches status drift immediately after implementation instead of letting the durable plan become fiction.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Use when executing approved plan manifests across multiple sessions or phases, especially when the user asks for TDD and self-verification.

lessonsLearned.lesson: Scheduled jobs are easiest to evolve when the dispatcher only routes and each job reference is self-contained. Add executable wiring tests that assert config entries, reference files, dispatcher mentions, and whitelist semantics together so a new job cannot be half-registered.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Apply when adding maintenance jobs, auto-fixes, or dispatcher-managed workflows to the agent ecosystem.

lessonsLearned.lesson: Framework documentation that defines routing, trust levels, and zone boundaries should have executable wiring tests, not only human review. A regression test that checks the bootstrap, folder conventions, content-routing, system-design, and document-standards docs together catches partial documentation updates before a capability ships.
lessonsLearned.applicability: Use when a new capability changes multiple framework docs or cross-skill operating rules. Treat doc wiring as behavior and test for the critical phrases/contracts.
